channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: Painstakingly Restored Mirror’s Edge Prototype Reveals My New Favorite Video Game Quote
item.content:encoded:
An early Mirror’s Edge build proves why silence is golden. April is reportedly going to be filled with tons of cool game reveals. And Nvidia has done irreparable harm to the DLSS brand with its slop-face demo. It’s your Morning Checkpoint for April 8, 2026, where I am listening to the Gran Turismo 3 start menu music after I was recently served up this cool remix which I sincerely hope wasn’t made by AI.

Is Marathon really a good game for uncs? Almost every engagement between my fellow olds and I with other players says no. And now they’ve nerfed my boy. I would rather die by the knife than another shotgun blast or heat-scope snipe.

Softsoundd on YouTube recently put in the work to make an early PS3 version of the cult-hit game from nine months before its release in 2008 playable (via Eurogamer). “After extensive reverse engineering and hundreds of fixes, including repairing the broken EBOOT fSELF format, stubbing 306 corrupted functions, and resolving Unreal Engine package bugs, the build is finally playable!” they explained.

It’s an uglier version of the game and there’s not a ton of special stuff going on here. But it does feature a storm drain time trial that’s not in the retail release and a more chatty Faith at the start of the game. Instead of the confident silence she exudes in the full game, here Faith explains the world of Mirror’s Edge like a forgotten ’90s sci-fi gem. “In this city of mirrors, the edge is where you still have a choice.” I love it.

Expect the new Star Fox game to be revealed this month

Leaker NateTheHate recently affirmed that it would be tweeted out or appear in a Nintendo Today app post at some point in April.

PC Gamer surveyed its readers and found they hate DLSS 5

The question was simple: Could Nvidia convince you to use DLSS 5? Over 70 percent of respondents said no, with a third saying they were fundamentally opposed to the controversial slop face tech on ethical grounds.

Nickelodeon reveals a new tennis game

Nickelodeon Extreme Tennis: Next! features SpongeBob, the Ninja Turtles, and more in the animation company’s latest attempt to muscle in on Nintendo’s party game turf. Unlike Mario Kart and Mario Tennis, Nickelodeon Extreme Tennis will be on PS5, Xbox Series X/S, and PC as well as Switch.

Our first look at the Assassin’s Creed Black Flag remake is coming soon

That’s according to Insider Gaming, which reports a full reveal is planned for mid-April. The timing feels right. A new Title 1.1.10 update for Assassin’s Creed Shadows added a clue that fans have decoded to be a direct tease for Black Flag Resynced.

Stellar Blade 2 won’t be shown until it’s ready

Don’t expect any early peaks at Shift Up’s next sci-fi Souls-lite. “Our team is currently working very passionately on the production, and we would like to present it once it has reached a satisfactory quality. Rather than sharing it prematurely, we believe it is best to inform everyone once everything is fully prepared,” CEO Kim Hyung-tae recently told 4Gamer (via TheGamer).

Physical game publisher Limited Run games throws its old CEO under the bus

The company recently announced a “renewed fan-first focus” following the departure of cofounder Josh Fairhurst. Over the past week, Limited Run has reportedly been emailing customers about unexpected delays, blaming the shifting schedules on previous estimates that were “overly optimistic” and “aggressive” delivery dates from the prior administration.

ICYMI:

  • Avalanche’s ambitious fantasy game from the Xbox 360 era was canceled in the worst way
  • Give Star Fox his own Netflix anime spin-off
  • Marathon‘s first month was a roller coaster ride of vibes

Watch this:



https://kotaku.com/assassins-creed-black-flag-remake-star-fox-reveal-2000685729
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Forza Horizon 6 Is On Track To Be The Best One Yet
item.content:encoded:
I’m not entirely sure when it happened, but at some point in the last decade, Forza Horizon quietly became Xbox’s best ongoing franchise, eclipsing Halo, Gears of War, and other big names. There hasn’t been a bad Forza Horizon game yet, and you could argue that each new entry has been better than the last.

After playing a few hours of Forza Horizon 6 via a preview build sent over by Xbox, I can confirm that the Japan-set Forza Horizon 6 seems likely to continue this trend.

Started from the bottom

Fans have long wanted Forza Horizon to make the leap to Japan, and later this year, that’s finally happening when Forza Horizon 6 launches on Xbox and PC (and later PS5). But before then, I got to check out the opening two hours of Horizon 6, including a bombastic and wild introduction that I won’t spoil here because Horizon intros are too cool to ruin like that. But if you want to see it, Xbox has posted it below:

However, after that over-the-top intro, Horizon 6 slows down a lot and offers up a more grounded narrative.

You aren’t the all-star hotshot racer from past entries. Instead, this time around, you play as a new character who isn’t even officially part of the Horizon event. Instead—and the preview focused on this—you’ll have to prove yourself and earn entry to the festival while also exploring the large open world of digital Japan. Don’t expect adoring fans or powerful supercars in these early hours. Instead, I spent most of my time in the Horizon 6 preview in a ’90s-era Toyota Celica GT-Four. It ain’t a fancy Ferrari, but it was damn fun to drive.

The real star of Forza Horizon 6, like in past entries, is the map. The open worlds crafted by developer Playground for past Horizon games have all been amazing, filled with incredible detail and loads of streets to speed down. With Japan, Playground has created what might end up being its best setting yet. It’s certainly the best-looking one, at the very least.

While I only had access to a few standard races in the preview, I was able to fully explore the massive open-world map, and it’s gorgeous. Japan has a load of visual variety. Tokyo is the biggest urban city center to appear in a Horizon game, and whipping and drifting through the place was like exploring a dazzling maze of concrete and glass. Elevated highways cut through and around the city, and following those will take you out to the countryside and smaller villages. Keep going, and you’ll find forests and even a large chunk of the digital world covered permanently in snow. I drove up a massive mountain, and looking out over the world from the top of it was breathtaking.  And yes, there are cherry blossom trees, and yes, their pink leaves are very pretty to look at while zipping by.

So much to do, so much to see…

As with past Horizon games, Forza Horizon 6 rewards cruising around and exploring. This time, you have different colorful mascot statues to destroy that earn you XP. These replace the fast-travel signs in past games that lowered the cost of using the feature. This time around, fast traveling to locations you’ve visited previously is free. As you would expect, there are also speed traps, drift zones, and other franchise staples dotted around the open world, too.

But I also discovered something new, a time trial event that I didn’t need to enter a menu to activate. I just drove through its trigger point and started setting a time. These types of open-world events offer up a small diversion organically and feel like a nice way to push even those players who are just vibing out to focus up for a moment.

Another new feature I encountered was a car for sale in a parking lot in a small village area of the map. These are randomly spawned-in aftermarket vehicles that you can buy for some credits, and they seem to replace the barn finds of past games. It’s another example of Forza Horizon 6 trying not to shower the player with free cars and celebrity rewards. See a cool car out there? Well, you gotta buy it, buddy, and money might not be as easy to come by as before, considering the preview build I played hints at having to do side jobs, like delivery, to pay for upgrades and new vehicles.

This is all okay with me, though, as my biggest issue with the Horizon games has long been the way they flood your garage with cars and your wallet with credits, robbing you of the classic racing game progression of crappy cars to better cars to great cars. It seems Horizon 6 is trying to rein the franchise in a bit, though its wild opening is a reminder that this is still a Horizon sequel, so expect some exciting shenanigans, too. After playing a few hours of Forza Horizon 6, I’m excited to hop into the full game when it launches on May 19 on Xbox and PC. Well, that’s one of its release dates, anyway….



https://kotaku.com/forza-horizon-6-is-on-track-to-be-the-best-one-yet-2000685747
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Players Are Losing Their Pokémon Trying To Transfer Them To Champions
item.content:encoded:
Pokémon Champions is out on Switch today, and its launch could be going better. Competitive players are up in arms about the battle simulator’s many restrictions and bugs, but one bug in particular may have ramifications that go beyond any one game, as some players are reporting that Pokémon they’ve attempted to transfer from the Home storage app to Champions are being caught in some kind of error, making it impossible to complete the transfer to Champions or send them back to the Home app.

Competitive Pokémon player Justin “AzazeL” Carris posted about the bug on X, and was met with responses from other players who have also found some of their favorite monsters in a weird limbo state. The Home error may pop up when transferring Pokémon over to Champions, whether that be in the Switch or mobile version of the app. There have been reports of this error code coming up when trying to transfer Pokémon from Home to various games over the years, so this issue predates Champions. However, in this case the app will register the affected Pokémon as “visiting” Champions, and as a result you cannot trade them back, essentially trapping the Pokémon in some kind of in-between place where they cannot be recovered or used in Champions.

Some folks have been able to resolve the issue by uninstalling and reinstalling Home, while others were given instructions to clear the game’s and system’s cache, but no one solution seems to be working for everyone at this time. We’ve reached out to The Pokémon Company and will update this story if we hear back.

Feeling pretty grateful that I didn’t experience this glitch myself. I was only able to transfer over a few of my favorites due to Champions’ limited roster, but some of those Pokémon I’ve had for years as they originate from the Game Boy Advance games so I’d really hate to lose them. Now I get to have normal, less stressful problems with Champions, such as asking why the hell Mega Raichu is in the game’s trailers but not the game itself.



https://kotaku.com/pokemon-champions-home-transfer-error-9007-2000685757
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
